title: Common Noise-Induced Group-Level Synchronization Between Uncoupled Groups of Oscillators
abstract: We investigate group-level synchronization between oscillator groups induced by common noise in the absence of inter-group coupling. Each group receives a common noise shared by all its oscillators and independent local noise inputs to individual oscillators. The same common noise is applied to all groups. The system is studied with both identical and nonidentical oscillators, and with and without intra-group coupling. In the nonidentical case, natural frequencies are drawn from the same distribution for both groups, making them statistically equivalent. Through numerical simulations of this system, we find that the degree of synchronization within each group, measured by the absolute value of a complex Kuramoto order parameter, typically shows significant temporal fluctuations. Importantly, the complex order parameters representing the collective oscillations of the groups synchronize when the groups are driven by the same common noise. By deriving a phase density evolution mapping, we analytically explain how this group-level synchronization is achieved in the absence of intra-group coupling.
summary: This work analyzes how shared common noise can synchronize the collective behavior of multiple groups of uncoupled oscillators. Using phase density mapping, the authors analytically prove that this group-level synchronization occurs even without direct coupling between the groups. This mechanism is significant for understanding emergent order in complex, noisy physical systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates how common noise can induce group-level synchronization between multiple uncoupled groups of limit-cycle oscillators.
main_result: Group-level synchronization is robustly achieved when groups share a common noise source, even in the absence of direct inter-group coupling. This synchronization is analytically explained via phase density evolution mapping.
method: The study employs numerical simulations of stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and derives analytical explanations using phase density evolution mappings and overlap fraction analysis.
model_or_system: The system consists of multiple groups of coupled or uncoupled limit-cycle oscillators, modeled by phase dynamics governed by SDEs incorporating common and local noise components.
key_observables: Complex Kuramoto order parameter ($Z_g$), group-level synchronization measure ($d_{12}(t)$), and the overlap fraction of phase densities ($F^c_{\omega,n}$).
important_parameters: Noise strength ($\sigma$), noise correlation coefficient ($c_{in}$), intra-group coupling strength ($K$), and natural frequency heterogeneity ($\sigma_\omega$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on weak coupling and weak additive white noise, allowing reduction to a phase-only system, and uses approximations like the Euler-Maruyama method for discretization.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of order parameters ($R(t)$) and phase density functions ($f_{g,n}(	heta)$) for various coupling and noise regimes, demonstrating convergence and overlap increase.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the synchronization phenomenon across four regimes: uncoupled nonidentical, coupled identical, coupled nonidentical, and the general case, using both time-domain order parameters and phase-space density evolution.
why_interesting: The mechanism of noise-induced collective behavior in uncoupled systems, particularly the emergence of global order from shared noise, provides insights into complex dynamics relevant to open quantum systems and pattern formation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Order parameter R of a group of uncoupled identical oscillators (σω = 0, K = 0): (a) R(t) for cin = 1.0 and cin = 0.9 (b) R(t) for cin = 0.5, cin = 0.2, and cin = 0.0. Simulations of (a) and (b) are performed with σ = 0.2 and ∆(θ) = −sin θ. (c) ⟨¯R⟩and ⟨σR⟩as a function of cin with ∆(θ) = −sin θ. (d) ⟨¯R⟩and ⟨σR⟩as a function of cin with ∆(θ) = 1 −cos θ. Initial values of phases are randomly selected from [0, 2π). Diﬀerent random seeds are used for generating the noise inputs and the initial phases for diﬀerent simulations. For the details, see the text.

FIG. 2. Two groups of uncoupled identical oscillators (σω = 0, K = 0): (a) With diﬀerent common noise inputs to the groups (dξ1c ̸= dξ2c): asynchronous behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for cin = 0.5. (b) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (a). (c) With the same common noise input to the groups (dξ1c = dξ2c): synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for cin = 0.5. (d) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (c). Simulations of (a)-(d) are performed with σ = 0.2 and ∆(θ) = −sin θ. (e) ⟨rx⟩as a function of cin with ∆(θ) = −sin θ. (f) ⟨rx⟩ as a function of cin with ∆(θ) = 1 −cos θ. In (a) and (c), initial values of phases are randomly selected from [0, 2π) for group 1 (red curve) and from [0, 0.2π) for group 2 (green curve)....

FIG. 3. Two groups of uncoupled nonidentical oscillators (σω ̸= 0, K = 0) with the same common noise input to the groups (dξ1c = dξ2c): (a) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for σω = 0.02, σ = 0.2, and cin = 1. (b) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (a). (c) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for σω = 0.1, σ = 0.2, and cin = 1. (d) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (c). ⟨¯R⟩and ⟨σR⟩as functions of cin for (e) σω = 0.02 and (f) σω = 0.1. ⟨rx⟩as functions of cin for (g) σω = 0.02 and (h) σω = 0.1. ∆(θ) = −sin θ for all the ﬁgures. Initial conditions are as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. Two groups of coupled identical oscillators (σω = 0, K > 0) with the same common noise input to the groups (dξ1c = dξ2c) : (a) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) with intermittent desynchrony for K = 0.02, σ = 0.2, and cin = 0.5. (b) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (a). (c) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for K = 0.04, σ = 0.2, and cin = 0.4. (d) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (c). ⟨¯R⟩and ⟨σR⟩as functions of cin for (e) σ = 0.2 and (f) σ = 0.4. ⟨rx⟩as functions of cin for (g) σ = 0.2 and (h) σ = 0.4. ∆(θ) = −sin θ for all the ﬁgures. Initial conditions are as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Two groups of coupled nonidentical oscillators (σω ̸= 0, K > 0) with the same common noise input to the groups (dξ1c = dξ2c): (a) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) with intermittent desynchrony for σω = 0.1, K = 0.18, σ = 0.2, and cin = 0.5. (b) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (a). (c) Synchronized behaviors of R1(t) and R2(t) for σω = 0.1, K = 0.18, σ = 0.4, and cin = 0.5. (d) d12(t) and ˜rx(t) for (c). ⟨¯R⟩and ⟨σR⟩as functions of cin for (e) σ = 0.2 and (f) σ = 0.4. ⟨rx⟩as functions of cin for (g) σ = 0.2 and (h) σ = 0.4. ∆(θ) = −sin θ for all the ﬁgures. Initial conditions are as in Fig. 2.
